figures.caption: Figure 2: Where to write: Mixture Memory Updates. (a) CUT3R overwrites all state tokens at every timestep. (b) TTT3R applies a dense per-token gate to modulate how much to write, but still updates every token. (c–d) MeMix enables where-to-write updates via Mixture Memory: only a subset of memory patches/tokens are written while the rest are exactly preserved, and it can be
Table 7: Video Depth Estimation. We evaluate scale-invariant and metric depth accuracy on KITTI [50], Sintel [52], and Bonn [51] datasets. Methods that require global alignment are denoted as "GA"

Figure 9: Visualization of Estimated Camera Trajectories – Long Sequence. We compare • GT, • baseline trajectories, and • their corresponding MeMix variants across three backbones: CUT3R, TTT3R, and TTSA3R. Across different backbones, the MeMix variants generally stay closer to the ground-truth trajectories and exhibit reduced drift over long sequences.

Figure 6: Evaluation on Video Depth Estimation. We compare CUT3R, TTT3R and TTSA3R with their w/o MeMix version under long input streams. MeMix generally improves depth estimation quality for input lengths ranging from 50 to 1000 frames. Notably, the outcomes largely depend on the capacity of the original model.

Figure 3: Overview of MeMix. A ViT encoder encodes each frame to tokens Xt, which interact with state tokens St−1 through a dual-stream cross-attention decoder to produce predictions Yt and candidate state Ŝt. MeMix computes dot scores between Ŝt and Xt, selects Bottom-k patches to construct a binary mask Mt, updating only Bottom-K patches. Decoded image tokens Yt are fed to the prediction head for output.

Figure 8: Visualization of distinct strategies. We employ Top-k and Bottom-k strategies separately, and tally the state tokens that are updated in each input frame. Representative examples show that Bottom-k not only achieves a higher update frequency, but also yields a more balanced update distribution across all state tokens.
